VII. The Agastis.
{3 Agastya, Dardhacliyuta, or Idhmavaheti. 3 Agastya, Dardhachyuta, Somavaheti.

There are other lists of much greater extent, which may become useful in time for chronological calculations. In them the first branch of the Bhrigus, the Vatsas, count 73 names ; among them such names as Saunakayanah (8), Pailah (13), Paingalayanah (14), Paninih (29), Valmikayah (30). The Vidas comprise 13, the Arshtishenas 8, the Yaskas 20, the Mitrayus 11, the Vainyas 3, and the Sunakas 9 names. It would occupy too much space to print these lists here.
In order to prove that these lists were not merely arbitrary compositions, their practical bearing on two very important acts of the ancient Brahmanic society, c c 386 GOTRAS.
the consecrating of the sacrificial fires, and marriage, should be borne in mind.

When the fire is to be consecrated, Agni Havyavahana, the god who carries the libations to heaven, must be invoked. This invocation or invitation of Agni, is called pravara.1 Agni himself or the fire is called Arsheya, the offspring of the Rishis, because the Rishis first lighted him at their sacrifices. He is the Hotri as well as the Adhvaryu among the gods. Like the Hotri and Adhvaryu priests, he is supposed to invite the gods to the sacrifice, and to carry himself the oblation to the seat of the immortals. When therefore a Brahman has his own fire consecrated, he wishes to declare that he is as worthy as his ancestors to offer sacrifices, and he invites Agni to carry his oblations to the gods as he did for his ancestors. The names of these ancestors must then be added to his invitation, and thus the invitation or invocation of the ancestors came to be called pravara. For instance, if a Brahman belongs to the family of the Mandukeyas, he must know that the Mandukeyas belong to the Vatsas, and that the Yatsas are descended from Bhrigu, and invoke five ancestors. He must, therefore, like all members of the Vatsa-gotra, invoke Agni by the names of Bhargava, Chyavana, Apnavana, Aurva, and Jamadgna. If he belong to the family of Yajnavalkya, a branch of the Kusikas, descendants of Visvamitra, he must invoke Agni by the name of Visvamitra, Devarata and Udala. This, at least, is GOTRAS. 387 the rule laid down in the Baudhayana-sutra, with which the Asvalayana-sutra coincides, except that it does not mention the Yajnavalkyas as a subdivision of the Kusikas. This custom was known at the time of the composition of the Brahmanas, and we have no reason to doubt that ever since the first establishment of Vedic sacrifices, the forty-nine families preserved the tradition of their sacred pedigree, and that their genealogies possess a certain historical value.1 This is confirmed still further if we consider the ancient Brahmanic marriage laws. To marry a woman belonging to the same Gotra, or having the same Pravara, was considered incest, and visited with severe penance. Asvalayana (xii. 15.) says : " Asamanapravarair vivahah." "Marriage takes place with persons who have not the same Pravara, i. e. who do not invoke the same Rishis as their ancestors." Apastamba says: "Sagotraya duhitaram na prayachchhet," " Thou shalt not give thy daughter to a man belonging to the same Gotra or 'family." Yajnavalkya says : " Aroginim bhratrimatirn asamanarshagotrajam udvahet." " Let a man marry a woman who is free from disease, who has brothers, and who is not the daughter of a man having the same ancestors and belonging to the same Gotra as himself." In each case severe punishments are threatened if a man 1 Thus we read in the Srauta-sutras of the Manavas, that the Dikshita must say his name, the name of his Gotra, of his father, grandfather, and great grandfather ; a custom which, if observed as a sacred law, must have preserved a genealogical knowledge for many generations. ^tf%rfrS*nTCTf%f7f ^T*T "^WTf^l ^T~ c c 2 388 GOTRAS.

transgress these rules knowingly, or even unknowingly. There are some special rules with regard to marriage, which differ again according to different Sutras; of which the following, taken from Asvalayana, may serve as a specimen :
1. Persons who have the same Pravara must not intermarry. Hence a Parasara must not marry the daughter of a Parasara.
2. Persons belonging to the same Gotra must not intermarry. Hence a Visvamitra must not marry the daughter of a Visvamitra.

3. There are exceptions to this rule among the Bhrigus and Angi rasas. As a general rule, persons are called sagotra, if but one of the Rishis whom they invoke is the same. Hence an Upamanyu must not marry the daughter of a Parasara, because the name of Vasishtha occurs in the tryarsheya pravara of both. But the three Gotras of the Bhrigus, from the Syaitas to the &unakas, may intermarry. The first four Gotras of the Bhrigus must not, neither the six first Gotras of the Gotamas. The Prishadasvas, Mudgalas, Yishnuvriddhas, Kanvas, Agastyas, Haritas, Sankritis, Kapis and Yaskas may intermarry among themselves, and with the Jamadagnyas, &c. Dhirghatamas', on the contrary, Auchathyas and Kakshivats are to be considered as members of one Gotra, nor are marriages allowed between the Bharadvajagnivesis, Rikshas, £unga-&aisiris, (or Sungas, Saisiris), Katas, and, according to some, the Gargas.

It is clear from this that the science of genealogy, being so intimately connected with the social and ecclesiastical system of the Brahmans, must have been studied with great care in India, and that the BRAHMANAS. 389 genealogical lists which have been preserved to us in ancient works represent something real and historical.

After we have thus gained an insight into the system by which the Brahmanas were handed down from generation to generation, we now return to a consideration of the literary merits of these works. The Brahmanas represent no doubt a most interesting phase in the history of the Indian mind, but judged by themselves, as literary productions, they are most disappointing. No one would have supposed that at so early a period, and in so primitive a state of society, there could have risen up a literature which for pedantry and downright absurdity can hardly be matched anywhere. There is no lack of striking thoughts, of bold expressions, of sound reasoning, and curious traditions in these collections. But these are only like the fragments of a torso, like precious gems set in brass and lead. The general character of these works is marked by shallow and insipid grandiloquence, by priestly conceit, and antiquarian pedantry. It is most important to the historian that he should know how soon the fresh and healthy growth of a nation can be blighted by priestcraft and superstition. It is most important that we should know that nations are liable to these epidemics in their youth as well as in their dotage. These works deserve to be studied as the physician studies the twaddle of idiots, and the raving of madmen. They will disclose to a thoughtful eye the ruins of faded grandeur, the memories of noble aspirations. But let us only try to translate these works into our own language, and we shall feel astonished that c c 3 390 BRAHMANAS.

human language and human thought should ever have been used for such purposes. The following is a small specimen, and it has not been chosen to give an unfavourable idea of the Brahmanas. It is the beginning of the Aitareya-brahmana, and explains a sacrificial act in itself full of meaning. Originally the Dikshaniya, as this ceremony is called, was meant to represent, by simple and natural emblems, the new birth through which a man, on his first admission to the sacrifice, was believed to enter a new life. Let us see what became of this act in the hands of the Brahmans.
Aitareya-brahmana. — Dikshaniya.
Agni is the first among the Gods, Vishnu the last.1 Between them stand all the other deities.

They offer a Purolasa to Agni and Vishnu which has been prepared for the Dikshaniya in eleven jars.2 \3rTT:il The commentator says that the gods among whom Agni and Vishnu are the first and last, are the gods to whom prayers are offered at the ceremonies belonging to the Agnishtoma. There are 12 prayers (sastra), and the first is addressed to Agni (bhiir Agnir jyotih) ; the last, which is an agnimaruta, contains a verse in praise of Vishnu (Vishnor nu kam). See Kaushitaki-brahmana, viii. 1. This passage proves nothing as to the relative dignity of Agni and Vishnu. In the Kaush.-br. Agni is called avararddhya, Vishnu pararddhyas, and the Com. explains these terms as signifying the first in the former, and the first in the latter half.

A pui ojasa is a baked flour cake (pakvah pishtapindah), and nirvap, to strew, means originally to take four handfuls of rice BRAHMAN AS. 391 They offer it indeed to all the deities of this ceremony, without any difference.1 For Agni is all the deities, Vishnu is all the deities.2 They are the two extremities of the sacrifice, Agni and Vishnu. Thus when men offer the Purolasa to Agni and Vishnu, they worship the deities at both ends.3 Here they say, if there be a Purojasa prepared in eleven jars, and there be two gods, Agni and Vishnu, what rule is there for the two, or what division ? 4 The Puro]asa of eight jars belongs to Agni, for the Gayatri verse consists of eight syllables, and the Gayatri is Agni's metre. That of three jars belongs to Vishnu, for Vishnu strode thrice through this universe. This is their rule here, and this the division.5 from the cart and throw them into a winnowing basket. Here, however, it means the offering of the oblation which has been prepared in that manner. The original meaning of Diksha is said to be " shaving or cleansing."
^^f%3rwr %^f%ffcf f^^ff^^^rr l^r^r^ c c 4 392 BKAHMANAS.

He who thinks himself without wealth, may offer a Charu in ghee (clarified butter).1 On this earth no one succeeds who has no wealth.2 The ghee in the Charu, is the milk of the woman, the grains belong to the man ; both together are a pair. Thus the Charu increases him by this very pair with progeny and cattle, so that he may prosper.3 He who knows this is increased with progeny.4 He who performs the New-moon and Full-moon sacrifices, has commenced with the sacrifice and with the gods.5 After having sacrificed with the new moon or full-moon oblation, he may perform the Diksha on the same oblation and the same sacrificial seat.6 ^hWI^t rpR^rr mjfir: "R^reffr wraN The commentator tries to show that the Darsa-piirna-masa sacrifices are connected with all other rites. Although the Soma sacrifice is not a modification of the Dai sa-purna-masa. still the Ishtis, as, for instance, the Dikshaniya and PrayanJya, are, and they form part of the Soma sacrifice. The Agnihotra also, with all its parts, does not follow the rule of the D. P., but it has reference to the Ahavaniya and the other sacred fires, and these fires must be placed by means of the Pavamana-ishti. Now, as all the Ishtis are modifications of the D. P., the relation is established ; and therefore the D. P. may be called the beginning of sacrifices.

The commentator says: havih means the sacrifice, and barhih BRAHMANAS. 393 This is one Diksha.1 The Hotri must recite seventeen Samidheni verses.2 The Prajapati, the Lord of the World, is seventeenfold, the months are twelve, and the seasons five, by putting the Hemanta and Sisira seasons as one. So much is the year, and the year is Prajapati.3 He who knows this prospers by those verses which reside in Prajapati.4 The sacrifice went away from the gods. They wished to find it by means of the Ishtis. The Ishtis are called Ishtis because with them they wished (ish, to wish) to find it.5 They found it.6 means the same, and he takes the two locatives in the sense of "after this new moon and full moon sacrifice has been performed."

我注意到提供的英文原文似乎存在編碼或轉錄錯誤。「&andil&h」並非有效的英文或梵文，無法準確翻譯。 如果這是指梵文人名或文獻的參考條目，建議您提供清晰的原始文本。例如，若應為 Kasyapa（迦葉波）、Asita（阿西陀）、Daivaleti 等人名，我可以準確翻譯。 請確認並提供正確的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行翻譯。
VI. 瓦西什塔族
45. 婆濕笈多（Vasishthah）為婆濕笈多（Vasishtheti）。
46. 烏帕曼雅瓦赫（Upamanyavah）、瓦西什塔（Vasishtha）、阿巴拉德瓦蘇（Abharadvasu）、因陀羅普拉瑪德提（lndrapramadeti）。
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」實際上是梵文注解（Parasarah 是梵文文獻，Vasishtha 和 Parasaryeti 是梵文人名），而非英文。 根據您的規則第2項「只翻譯英文原文；附上的德文僅供消歧義參考，不要翻譯德文」，此段落應不在翻譯範圍內。 若您有穆勒1873年《宗教學導論》的英文原文段落需要翻譯，請提供標準英文文本，我將按照您的規則進行專業翻譯。
48. 昆地那（Kundinah）3 瓦西什塔、邁特拉瓦魯那、考恩迪尼耶蒂（Vasishtha, Maitravaru- na, Kaundinyeti）。

The Agastis are mentioned in the Rig-Veda as the descendants of the Mitra and Varuna, and they seem to have been a powerful race in the early history of the Aryan nations. They appear in the mythology of the Greeks as the Aloadæ (Ἀλωάδαι), the sons of Aloeus, and in the mythology of the Italians as the Aloades. According to the Greek accounts, they were giants of enormous strength, who piled Pelion on Ossa in order to scale the heavens; but in the Indian legendary accounts, the Agastis are conceived of as righteous men, sages, and performers of good works. The difference between the Greek and Indian conception of the Agastis may perhaps be explained by the fact that the Greeks, in adopting these legends, had lost the true meaning of the name, and, mistaking it for a word signifying "great" or "mighty" (ἄλας), transformed the divine sages and righteous men of Indian tradition into giants of fabulous strength and stature. The Sanskrit root *ghas* signifies "to devour," or "to consume," and *Agastis* may therefore be interpreted as "the devourers," or beings who overcome and conquer all obstacles; and hence we may understand how, in the hands of the Greeks, they became transformed into giants of superhuman power.
{3 阿迦斯堤耶（Agastya）、達爾陀吒尤塔（Dardhacliyuta）或伊德瑪瓦赫提（Idhmavaheti）。3 阿迦斯堤耶（Agastya）、達爾陀吒尤塔（Dardhachyuta）、蘇摩瓦赫提（Somavaheti）。

還有其他篇幅龐大得多的名單，日後可能在年代學計算中派上用場。其中婆利古（Bhrigus）的第一個分支瓦刹族（Vatsas）列舉了七十三個名字；其中包括蘇那卡亞那（Saunakayanah，第八）、派拉（Pailah，第十三）、派恩迦拉亞那（Paingalayanah，第十四）、帕尼尼（Paninih，第二十九）、瓦爾米卡亞（Valmikayah，第三十）等名字。吠陀族（Vidas）包含十三個，阿爾什提舍那族（Arshtishenas）八個，亞斯卡族（Yaskas）二十個，彌陀羅尤族（Mitrayus）十一個，瓦因亞族（Vainyas）三個，蘇那卡族（Sunakas）九個名字。在此刊印這些名單將佔用過多篇幅。
為了證明這些名單並非僅是武斷的編纂，我們需要檢視它們對古代婆羅門社會兩項極其重要的行為所具有的實際影響， cc 386 GOTRAS.
祭祀之火的奉獻禮儀及婚禮，應當銘記於心。

當火焰要被祝聖時，運送祭祀供物到天堂的提婆阿格尼（Agni Havyavahana）必須被祈請。這種對阿格尼的祈請或邀請被稱為普拉瓦拉（pravara）。阿格尼本身或火焰被稱為阿舍亞（Arsheya），即仙人（Rishis）的後裔，因為仙人在他們的祭祀中首先點燃了他。他在眾神中既是火祭司（Hotri）也是執祭司（Adhvaryu）。如同火祭司和執祭司，他被認為在祭祀中邀請眾神，並親自將供物傳遞到不朽者的居所。因此，當一個婆羅門（Brahman）為自己的火焰祝聖時，他希望宣示自己與祖先一樣有資格獻祭，並邀請阿格尼如他為祖先所做的那樣，將他的供物傳遞給眾神。這些祖先的名字必須加入他的祈請中，因此祖先的祈請或邀請便被稱為普拉瓦拉。例如，如果一個婆羅門屬於曼杜基亞（Mandukeyas）家族，他必須知曉曼杜基亞屬於瓦茨亞（Vatsas），而瓦茨亞是從布里古（Bhrigu）傳承下來的，並祈請五位祖先。因此，他必須如同所有瓦茨亞宗族（Vatsa-gotra）的成員一樣，以巴爾加瓦（Bhargava）、奇亞瓦納（Chyavana）、阿普納瓦納（Apnavana）、奧爾瓦（Aurva）和加瑪德格納（Jamadgna）的名字祈請阿格尼。如果他屬於雅阿納瓦爾基亞（Yajnavalkya）家族，為庫西卡（Kusikas）的一支，是毗濕瓦密多羅（Visvamitra）的後裔，他必須以毗濕瓦密多羅、德瓦拉塔（Devarata）和烏達拉（Udala）的名字祈請阿格尼。至少在包達亞納經（Baudhayana-sutra）中是如此規定的，而阿斯瓦拉亞納經（Asvalayana-sutra）與之相符，除了它不將雅阿納瓦爾基亞稱為庫西卡的一個分支。這個習俗在梵書（Brahmanas）編纂時期就已為人所知，我們沒有理由懷疑，自從吠陀祭祀首次確立以來，四十九個家族便保持著他們神聖血統的傳統，他們的譜系具有一定的歷史價值。如果我們考慮古代婆羅門的婚姻法律，這一點就更加得到確證。與同一宗族（Gotra）或具有相同普拉瓦拉（Pravara）的女性通婚被視為亂倫，並受到嚴厲的懺悔。阿斯瓦拉亞納（xii. 15.）說：「不同普拉瓦拉者結婚。」意即「與那些普拉瓦拉不同、不祈請同樣祖先仙人的人結婚。」阿帕斯丹巴（Apastamba）說：「不要將你的女兒嫁給同一宗族或『家族』的人。」雅阿納瓦爾基亞說：「一個男人應當娶一個無病、有兄弟、且其父親的祖先和宗族與自己不同的女人。」在每種情況下，若一個男人違反這一規則，都會受到嚴厲的懲罰。因此，我們在瑪納瓦人（Manavas）的斯羅陶塔經（Srauta-sutras）中讀到，受祝聖者（Dikshita）必須說出他的名字、他的宗族（Gotra）、父親、祖父和曾祖父的名字；這個習俗，若作為聖法被遵守，必定會保存許多代人的系譜知識。
故意或甚至無意地違反這些規則。關於婚姻有一些特殊的規則，這些規則根據不同的《經文》（Sutras）而有所不同；以下摘自《阿濕婆臘耶那》（Asvalayana）的規則可作為範例：
具有相同普拉瓦拉（Pravara）的人不得通婚。因此，帕剎羅（Parasara）不得娶帕剎羅之女。
2. 屬於同一 Gotra 的人不得通婚。因此，毗濕瓦密多羅（Visvamitra）不得娶毗濕瓦密多羅（Visvamitra）的女兒。

3. 布里古族（Bhrigus）和安吉剌薩族（Angi rasas）中存在此規則的例外。作為一般性規則，若一個人所祭祀的仙人（Rishis）中有至少一位相同，則此人被稱為同姓氏族（sagotra）。因此，烏帕曼紐（Upamanyu）不得與帕剎迦拉（Parasara）之女成婚，因為瓦西什塔（Vasishtha）之名同時出現在雙方的三仙讚頌（tryarsheya pravara）中。然而，布里古族的三個氏族，從敘亞依塔族至舒那卡族，可以通婚。布里古族的前四個氏族不得通婚，哥達馬族的前六個氏族亦然。普里沙達斯瓦族、穆德迦拉族、毘笯濕努婆利達族、康瓦族、阿迦斯蒂亞族、哈里塔族、三克里蒂族、卡比族及亞斯卡族可以彼此通婚，並可與雅馬達格尼亞族等通婚。反之，迪爾迦塔馬斯族、奧恰塔亞族及卡克希瓦特族應被視為一個氏族的成員，婆羅多瓦日·阿格尼韋西族、瑞克沙族、松迦–賽希里族（或松迦族、賽希里族）、卡塔族，以及據某些說法，迦爾迦族之間不允許通婚。
從這一點可以清楚看出，由於族譜學與婆羅門的社會及教會制度密切相連，該學問在印度必然得到極為認真的研究，而保存在古代著作中的婆羅門族譜表（BRAHMANAS. 389）代表了真實且具有歷史意義的事物。

在我們如此深入了解婆羅門書（Brahmanas）如何世代相傳的制度之後，我們現在轉而考察這些著作的文學價值。婆羅門書無疑代表了印度心智歷史上最有趣的一個階段，但單就其本身作為文學作品而言，它們最令人失望。沒有人會想到在如此早期、如此原始的社會狀態下，竟然會產生一部文獻，其繁瑣和荒謬之程度幾乎無處可尋。這些文集中不乏卓越的思想、大膽的表達、健全的推理和奇異的傳統。但這些不過如同軀體的碎片，如同鑲嵌在黃銅和鉛中的珍貴寶石。這些著作的總體特徵以膚淺而乏味的誇大辭藻、祭司的自負和古物學式的繁瑣為標誌。對於歷史學家而言，最為重要的是他應當認識到一個民族清新健康的發展何其迅速就能被祭司專制與迷信所摧毀。我們應當認識到民族在年輕和衰老的年月都易受此類流行症的侵襲，這最為重要。這些著作值得被研究，如同醫生研究白癡的胡言亂語和瘋子的呢喃夢囈。它們將向思慮周密的眼光披露褪色宏偉的廢墟、高尚志向的記憶。但讓我們只是試著將這些著作翻譯成我們自己的語言，我們就將感到驚奇。

人類語言與人類思想竟被用於此等目的，實令人遺憾。以下是一個小範例，它的選擇並非為了給予婆羅門經（Brahmanas）一個不利的印象。這是《愛達囉耶婆羅門經》（Aitareya-brahmana）的開篇，解釋了一項本身充滿意義的祭祀行為。原本，這項被稱為迪克沙尼亞（Dikshaniya）的儀式，本是藉由簡單自然的象徵物來代表靈魂的新生——人在初次被接納參與祭祀時，被認為由此進入新的生命。讓我們看看這項行為在婆羅門人的手中變成了什麼。
愛達雷亞梵書（Aitareya-brahmana）。——祭式啟蒙儀式（Dikshaniya）。
阿耆尼（Agni）是諸神中最初者，毘濕奴（Vishnu）是最後者。1 在他們之間矗立著所有其他的神祇。

他們向阿格尼（Agni）和毘濕奴（Vishnu）獻上一份普羅拉薩（Purolasa），該供物已按照迪克沙尼亞（Dikshaniya）的儀式在十一個罐子中準備好。2 \3rTT:il 評論家指出，在阿格尼史多瑪（Agnishtoma）儀式中進行的禮祭中，接受祈禱的諸神——其中阿格尼和毘濕奴分別是首尾之神——正是這些神祇。共有十二份祈禱文（sastra），第一份致敬阿格尼（bhiir Agnir jyotih）；最後一份是阿格尼瑪魯塔（agnimaruta），其中包含一句讚頌毘濕奴的經文（Vishnor nu kam）。參見《考希塔基梵書》（Kaushitaki-brahmana）第八卷第一節。本段落對於阿格尼與毘濕奴相對地位的尊貴性並無任何證明。在《考希塔基梵書》中，阿格尼被稱為「阿瓦拉德亞」（avararddhya），毘濕奴被稱為「帕拉德亞」（pararddhyas），註釋家解釋這些術語分別指前半部分和後半部分的首位。

一個普依‧歐賈薩（pui ojasa）是烤製的麵粉糕餅（pakvah pishtapindah），而「尼爾瓦普」（nirvap）意為撒散，原義是從大車上取四把米粒並將其投入篩選籃中。然而在此處，它指的是以這種方式準備的供物之奉獻。據說「迪克夏」（Diksha）的原義為「剃髮或淨化」。 他們確實將其奉獻於此儀式的所有提婆，毫無區別。因為阿格尼乃是所有提婆，毗濕奴亦是所有提婆。他們是祭祀的兩個極端，阿格尼和毗濕奴。是故當人們向阿格尼和毗濕奴奉獻普羅拉薩時，他們乃是敬拜位於兩端的提婆。在此有云，若有一個普羅賈薩以十一個罐子準備，而有兩位提婆阿格尼和毗濕奴，對此二者有何規則或何種劃分？八個罐子的普羅薩屬於阿格尼，蓋因迦耶特里詩句由八個音節組成，而迦耶特里乃是阿格尼的韻律。三個罐子的普羅薩屬於毗濕奴，蓋因毗濕奴曾三次踏遍此宇宙。此為彼等之規則，此為劃分。
我無法翻譯您提供的文本。這段內容似乎是亂碼或損毀的文檔掃描，並非可辨識的英文原文。 請提供清晰的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行繁體中文翻譯。

自認無財富者，可獻以酥油的查魯祭品（Charu）。¹ 在此大地上，無財富之人無人能成功。² 查魯中的酥油是女人的乳汁，穀物屬於男人；兩者共同構成一對。如此查魯藉此對偶增益他以子嗣和家畜，俾其得以興盛。³ 知此者以子嗣增益。⁴ 執行新月與滿月祭祀者，已與祭祀和眾神開始。⁵ 在以新月或滿月供物獻祭後，他可在同一供物與同一祭場上進行傳授儀式（Diksha）。⁶ 詮釋者試圖表明達莎-普爾那-馬薩祭祀（Darsa-pūrṇa-māsa）與所有其他儀式相關連。雖然蘇摩祭祀（Soma sacrifice）並非達莎-普爾那-馬薩祭祀的變體，但伊什提祭祀（Ishtis）——例如迪克沙尼亞（Dikshaniya）和普拉雅尼亞（Prayañjya）——乃是其變體，且構成蘇摩祭祀的部分。阿格尼霍特拉祭祀（Agnihotra）及其所有部分亦不遵循達莎-普爾那-馬薩祭祀的規則，但與阿哈瓦尼亞火祭和其他聖火有關，而這些聖火必須藉由帕瓦曼那-伊什提祭祀（Pavamana-ishti）來設置。既然所有伊什提祭祀皆為達莎-普爾那-馬薩祭祀的變體，其關係遂得以確立；因此達莎-普爾那-馬薩祭祀可被稱為祭祀之始。

評論者說：havih 意指祭祀，barhih 意指婆羅門經（Brahmanas）。393 這是一項 Diksha。1 Hotri（祭火僧）必須誦唸十七首 Samidheni 頌詩。2 世界之主 Prajapati（創造主）具有十七重性質，月份有十二，季節有五，將 Hemanta 與 Sisira 兩季合併為一。這就是一年的構成，而一年即是 Prajapati。3 誰若知曉此事，便藉由駐留在 Prajapati 中的那些頌詩而繁榮。4 祭祀從眾神那裡逃離。他們希望藉由 Ishtis 來尋獲它。Ishtis 之所以稱為 Ishtis，乃因他們以之來尋獲（ish，欲求）它。5 他們尋獲了它。6 涵義相同，而人在此處以「在此次新月與滿月祭祀被執行之後」的意義取用兩個處格。
